explanation: The 1s sit exactly where X and Z hold the same value: one group of four covers X=0,Z=0 and another covers X=1,Z=1. Inside each group the remaining variables take every combination, so they cancel out, leaving F = X'Z' + XZ (which is X XNOR Z).

Decimal: 1001101 = 64 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 77. Hex: group in 4s from the right, 100 1101 -> 0100 1101 = 4 D. Octal: group in 3s from the right, 1 001 101 -> 1 1 5. Grouping must always start at the right-hand (least significant) end; padding on the wrong side is the classic error here.

Each mode differs in how many levels of lookup are needed to reach the operand. Register direct (MOVE R1,R2) uses the register's contents itself; immediate (MOVE #X,R1) carries the literal inside the instruction; register indirect (MOVE (A0),R1) treats the register as a pointer and fetches memory at [A0]; indexed register indirect (MOVE X(A0),R1) adds the displacement X to the base register first, fetching from [A0]+X.

A T flip-flop toggles Q on each active clock edge only when T = 1, and holds Q unchanged when T = 0. Sample T at the rising edges only: T = 1,0,1,1 with Q starting at 0 gives Q = 1, 1, 0, 1. The trap is treating T as a level (making Q follow T) or reacting to the falling edge.

The NIST essential characteristics are on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service. Fixed capacity is the direct opposite of rapid elasticity, so C is the odd one out; A, B and D are all on the list verbatim.

Fetch, decode, execute is the definition of the instruction cycle (also called the fetch-execute cycle). Memory cycle and bus cycle name the lower-level timing of a single memory or bus access, not the whole per-instruction sequence.

Virtualization inserts a hypervisor between hardware and guests, letting it multiplex one physical CPU, memory and NIC into many isolated virtual machines. Encryption, load balancing and firewalls are all security or traffic functions and create no VMs.

Only C is stated as an advantage: the provider patches and upgrades the platform, so customers get updates automatically. A and B are worded as drawbacks (cloud lowers maintenance cost and gives near-unlimited scalability) and D is the manual work cloud backup services remove.

Customer control decreases along IaaS > PaaS > SaaS. In IaaS you manage OS and runtime, in PaaS only the app and data, and in SaaS you consume a finished application and control nothing below it, so SaaS gives the least infrastructure control. Private Cloud is a deployment model, not a service model, so it does not belong on this scale.

A bus is the shared set of address, data and control lines that carries signals between CPU, memory and I/O modules, so its role is communication. Storage is memory's job and execution is the CPU's job.

Answer unverified: the source PDF carries no key for this item, and two options are defensible. Under NIST, a community cloud (D) is infrastructure shared by several organizations with a common concern (compliance, mission, security policy), which matches 'shared among multiple organizations' most precisely. A public cloud (C) is also shared by many tenants, but it is defined as open to the general public rather than to a defined set of organizations. Expect D unless the paper's own slides define public cloud as the shared one.
Azure is Microsoft's cloud platform. Amazon sells AWS, Google sells GCP, IBM sells IBM Cloud.
IAM = Identity and Access Management: the service that authenticates principals (users, roles, services) and authorizes what each may do via policies.

Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext that is unreadable without the key. Compression also makes data unreadable to the eye but is fully reversible by anyone with no secret, so it protects nothing; backup and replication just copy the data.
A Service Level Agreement is the contract between the cloud service provider and its customer, defining committed service levels (uptime, response time) and the remedies if they are missed.

Serverless (FaaS) means the provider owns provisioning, scaling and patching of the servers; the customer only uploads code that runs on an event. Servers still exist, they are just invisible to the user, so A is the inverse of the model.

AWS Lambda runs event-triggered functions with no instance, OS or capacity to manage, which is the definition of serverless / Function as a Service. PaaS is the classic trap: it is close because the provider also manages the platform, but in PaaS you still deploy and keep a running application (and pay for its idle time), while Lambda bills only per invocation.

Edge computing processes data at or near the source instead of shipping it to a distant data centre, which cuts the round-trip time and therefore the latency. Bandwidth is the standard trap: edge does reduce bandwidth usage as a side effect, but latency reduction is the defining benefit and the keyed answer.
